subtopic_detail: Cannabis-induced psychosis observation off substance before antipsychotic
stem:
You are the early psychosis team registrar reviewing a 19-year-old man brought to the assessment unit by his family for a 2-week history of persecutory delusions, auditory hallucinations and disorganised behaviour. He has been smoking high-potency cannabis daily for the past 2 years and increased to multiple uses per day in the month prior to symptom onset. There is no family history of schizophrenia or other psychotic illness.

He has had no previous psychotic episodes, no suicidal or homicidal intent today, has no acute aggression, and has agreed voluntarily to inpatient assessment with his family supportive. Urine drug screen is positive for THC only. He has had a negative test for methamphetamine and other stimulants.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.0, HR 98, BP 124/78, RR 16, SpO2 99% room air, BMI 22
data_table.Examination: Mildly anxious, persecutory delusions, occasional auditory hallucinations, no thought disorder evident on examination, no extrapyramidal signs, no neurology deficit, no signs of intoxication or withdrawal currently
data_table.Investigations: Urine drug screen THC positive, all other substances negative; FBC, UEC, LFTs and TFTs normal; ECG normal; non-contrast CT head normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management plan?

options.text: Admit to an inpatient psychiatric unit voluntarily, cease all cannabis use with structured withdrawal support, observe for up to 4 weeks off cannabis, commence low-dose olanzapine 5 mg nocte if symptoms persist, and refer to early psychosis intervention service
options.rationale: Cannabis-induced psychotic disorder is managed with abstinence and observation; antipsychotic is started for persistent symptoms with planned weaning; early psychosis service follow-up captures transition to primary psychotic illness. (Source: RANZCP: Substance-induced Psychosis)

options.text: Commence clozapine 12.5 mg nocte titrating up over 2 weeks under the early psychosis service for treatment-resistant first-episode psychosis given the chronicity of cannabis use, with weekly FBC monitoring and cardiac monitoring per protocol
options.rationale: Clozapine is reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia after failure of two adequate antipsychotic trials; it is not first-line for any psychosis presentation. (Source: RANZCP: Substance-induced Psychosis)

options.text: Commit to involuntary detention under the Mental Health Act and arrange urgent electroconvulsive therapy for severe disorganised psychotic features, given the failure of community management to date and the chronic high-potency cannabis use pattern documented
options.rationale: He is engaging voluntarily without acute risk, so involuntary detention is inappropriate; ECT is not first-line for substance-induced psychosis. (Source: RANZCP: Substance-induced Psychosis)

options.text: Commence outpatient management with a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor for likely underlying mood and anxiety symptoms, with cognitive behavioural therapy referral and harm-minimisation cannabis counselling through the local drug and alcohol service
options.rationale: SSRI is not indicated for psychosis; the presentation requires psychiatric assessment, abstinence support and possible antipsychotic, not outpatient SSRI alone. (Source: RANZCP: Substance-induced Psychosis)

options.text: Refer urgently for schizoaffective disorder workup including mood charting and family interview, commence lithium 750 mg nocte after baseline renal and thyroid testing, and arrange outpatient psychiatric follow-up within the next 2 weeks for definitive diagnostic clarification
options.rationale: Schizoaffective diagnosis requires sustained mood episodes alongside psychotic symptoms; presenting picture is acute substance-induced psychosis. (Source: RANZCP: Substance-induced Psychosis)

explanation.summary: Cannabis-induced psychotic disorder requires abstinence and observation for at least 4 weeks before assigning a primary psychotic illness diagnosis. Inpatient admission supports cessation, monitoring and family education. Antipsychotic medication (low-dose olanzapine, risperidone or aripiprazole) is added if symptoms persist beyond 1 to 2 weeks or are severe. Early psychosis intervention services in Australia (such as headspace and EPPIC) provide structured follow-up; approximately 25 to 50% of cannabis-induced psychotic episodes transition to a primary psychotic illness, with younger age, longer duration of use and family history increasing risk.
key_points: Cease cannabis and observe 4 weeks before committing to primary psychotic illness diagnosis
Low-dose antipsychotic if symptoms persist or are severe
Approximately 25-50% transition to primary psychotic illness; early psychosis service follow-up
explanation.pearls: Synthetic cannabinoids (such as Spice and K2) cause severe agitation, seizures, hyperthermia, and rhabdomyolysis, with longer-duration psychotic symptoms than natural cannabis and resistance to standard urine drug screens.

subtopic_detail: Adult ADHD methylphenidate initiation with driving counselling
stem:
You are the consultant psychiatrist completing the new diagnosis of adult attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (combined presentation) in a 26-year-old IT engineer who has had longstanding inattention, impulsivity and emotional dysregulation. He drives 1 hour each way for his daily commute. There is no history of cardiac disease, no family history of sudden cardiac death, no current substance misuse, and no current depression or anxiety requiring treatment.

His baseline ECG is normal with QTc 410 ms and his blood pressure is 130/80. He is keen to start medication and understands stimulants are first-line. He has read consumer information and consented to monitoring.

data_table.Examination: Mildly restless, no thought disorder, no psychotic features, no neurology deficit, no signs of intoxication
data_table.Investigations: ECG normal QTc 410 ms, FBC, UEC, LFTs, TFTs all within normal limits; urine drug screen negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial pharmacotherapy plan and accompanying counselling?

options.text: Counsel that stimulants are contraindicated for daily drivers due to the impact on cognition and reaction time, and commence atomoxetine 40 mg daily as a non-stimulant first-line therapy with planned 6-week review of symptom response and tolerability
options.rationale: Stimulants generally improve, not impair, driving performance in adult ADHD; they remain first-line. Atomoxetine is second-line per RANZCP and Australian Practice Standards. (Source: RANZCP: ADHD in Adults)

options.text: Commence methylphenidate immediate-release 10 mg twice daily titrating weekly to symptom response and tolerability, counsel that driving while titrating is acceptable once side effects have stabilised, and arrange 4-week review with repeat BP, HR and side-effect assessment
options.rationale: Stimulants such as methylphenidate are first-line for adult ADHD per RANZCP; titration with side-effect monitoring is standard; driving advice is to take dose before driving once tolerated. (Source: RANZCP: ADHD in Adults)

options.text: Refer to a private neuropsychologist for cognitive testing and computerised continuous performance task to confirm diagnostic uncertainty before commencing any pharmacotherapy, with planned review after the formal cognitive assessment results are available
options.rationale: Diagnosis of adult ADHD is clinical per DSM-5 criteria; neuropsychological testing is an adjunct, not required to start treatment once diagnosis is established. (Source: RANZCP: ADHD in Adults)

options.text: Commence sertraline 50 mg daily for likely comorbid anxiety or depression as the most parsimonious treatment for adult ADHD-like presentations, given the substantial overlap of symptoms and the favourable side-effect profile of SSRIs as a first step
options.rationale: SSRIs are not effective for core ADHD symptoms; pharmacotherapy targets ADHD with stimulants or atomoxetine, with SSRI reserved for comorbid depression or anxiety. (Source: RANZCP: ADHD in Adults)

options.text: Commence clonidine 50 micrograms three times daily for adult ADHD, with planned 24-hour ambulatory blood pressure monitoring during titration and dose escalation, and a planned outpatient review at 4 weeks for symptom and side-effect assessment
options.rationale: Clonidine is third-line for ADHD, mainly used in children with tic disorders or sleep onset; not first-line in adult ADHD per Australian practice. (Source: RANZCP: ADHD in Adults)

explanation.summary: Adult ADHD diagnosis is clinical and based on DSM-5 criteria. Stimulant medications (methylphenidate or lisdexamfetamine) are first-line per RANZCP and the Australian ADHD Professionals Association guidelines, with non-stimulant atomoxetine or guanfacine as second-line, and clonidine third-line. Pre-treatment workup includes baseline BP, HR, ECG, and screening for cardiac and psychiatric risk factors. Driving is generally improved by stimulant treatment but patients should be counselled to take their dose before driving once tolerability is established. Comorbid depression, anxiety and substance use disorders are screened for and treated alongside.
key_points: Methylphenidate or lisdexamfetamine first-line per RANZCP
Atomoxetine and guanfacine second-line; clonidine third-line
Pre-treatment: baseline BP, HR, ECG; screen for cardiac and psychiatric comorbidity
explanation.pearls: Schedule 8 stimulant prescribing requires state-based authority (e.g. NSW Pharmaceutical Services Branch) for adults newly diagnosed with ADHD; clinicians must verify their state's authority requirements before prescribing.

sources.label: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

subtopic_detail: Native valve endocarditis empirical therapy with benzylpenicillin, flucloxacillin and gentamicin
stem:
You are the general medical registrar admitting a 45-year-old previously well man with 3 weeks of fevers, night sweats and progressive exertional dyspnoea. He has lost 4 kg, has new finger clubbing, and a recently noted soft early diastolic murmur at the left sternal edge. He has no intravenous drug use, no prosthetic valves, no recent dental procedure and no central venous access.

His chest X-ray is unremarkable. Echocardiography has been requested urgently and three sets of blood cultures have been collected at least 30 minutes apart in the emergency department prior to any antibiotic exposure. He is haemodynamically stable but has signs suggestive of mild congestive cardiac failure on examination today.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.2, HR 104, BP 124/68, RR 20, SpO2 96% room air, BMI 24
data_table.Examination: Soft early diastolic murmur at the left sternal edge grade 2/6, splinter haemorrhages bilateral fingernails, bilateral basal fine inspiratory crackles, hepatomegaly, no peripheral oedema, normal neurological examination, no Janeway lesions or Osler nodes
data_table.Investigations: WCC 13.6 neutrophilic, CRP 138, Hb 102 normocytic, urinalysis dipstick +2 protein and microscopic haematuria, ECG sinus tachycardia, chest X-ray clear; three sets of blood cultures sent; echocardiography requested urgently

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily for 2 weeks pending echocardiography results and blood culture sensitivities, with planned admission for definitive intravenous therapy only if echocardiography confirms vegetations on the valves
options.rationale: Endocarditis requires prolonged IV therapy; oral antibiotics are inappropriate as empirical therapy for suspected native valve endocarditis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic: Endocarditis)

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin 25 mg/kg loading dose then dose-adjusted monotherapy targeting a trough level of 15 to 20 mg/L, providing broad gram-positive cover including methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus and viridans group streptococci pending culture results
options.rationale: Vancomycin monotherapy is reserved for non-severe penicillin allergy or MRSA risk factors; combination therapy is standard for native valve endocarditis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic: Endocarditis)

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin 1.8 g four-hourly plus flucloxacillin 2 g four-hourly plus gentamicin 1 mg/kg eight-hourly, with continued cultures, urgent echocardiogram and infectious diseases consultation, narrowing on culture and sensitivity data
options.rationale: Australian first-line empirical therapy for community-acquired native valve endocarditis is benzylpenicillin plus flucloxacillin plus gentamicin to cover viridans streptococci, Staphylococcus aureus and enterococci. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic: Endocarditis)

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone 2 g daily monotherapy until culture results are available, given the broad gram-positive and gram-negative cover and convenient dosing schedule, with reassessment based on the formal echocardiogram and the eventual organism identified
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone monotherapy is inadequate empirical cover for native valve endocarditis; it misses enterococci and may underdose for Staphylococcus aureus. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic: Endocarditis)

options.text: Hold all antibiotics pending the echocardiogram and blood culture results in 48 hours, since premature empirical therapy may reduce the diagnostic yield of cultures and complicate identification of the responsible organism in subacute endocarditis presentations
options.rationale: Once three sets of blood cultures are taken at least 30 minutes apart, empirical therapy should be commenced rather than withholding antibiotics in clinically suspected endocarditis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic: Endocarditis)

explanation.summary: Empirical therapy for community-acquired native valve endocarditis in Australia is benzylpenicillin plus flucloxacillin plus gentamicin: benzylpenicillin for viridans group streptococci, flucloxacillin for Staphylococcus aureus, and low-dose gentamicin for enterococcal synergy. Three sets of blood cultures at least 30 minutes apart should be collected before antibiotics. Urgent transthoracic echocardiography (proceeding to transoesophageal if non-diagnostic) confirms diagnosis. Vancomycin replaces benzylpenicillin and flucloxacillin in non-severe beta-lactam allergy or known MRSA carriage. Treatment duration is typically 4 to 6 weeks depending on organism and clinical course.
key_points: BenPen + flucloxacillin + gentamicin is empirical Australian standard for native valve
Three blood cultures at least 30 min apart before antibiotics
Vancomycin substitutes in severe beta-lactam allergy or MRSA risk
explanation.pearls: Surgical indications in infective endocarditis include heart failure from valve dysfunction, persistent bacteraemia despite 5 to 7 days of appropriate antibiotics, large mobile vegetations greater than 10 mm with embolic events, and abscess or fistula formation.

subtopic_detail: Acute HIV seroconversion diagnosis HIV RNA when fourth-generation antigen antibody is negative
stem:
You are the sexual health clinic registrar reviewing a 32-year-old man with a 5-day history of fevers, sore throat, generalised lymphadenopathy, a non-pruritic maculopapular truncal rash and oral ulcers. He had condomless receptive anal intercourse with a new male partner 3 weeks ago. He has no previous diagnosis of HIV. Same-day point-of-care fourth-generation antigen-antibody HIV test is negative today. He has had no other recent illnesses and has had no other medications.

He is otherwise systemically well, haemodynamically stable, and not on any antiretroviral therapy currently. He has had no exposure to post-exposure prophylaxis in the past month and is keen to confirm or exclude HIV diagnosis today.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.0, HR 92, BP 122/76, RR 16, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Pharyngeal erythema with bilateral tonsillar exudate, generalised non-tender lymphadenopathy, fine non-blanching truncal maculopapular rash, oral aphthous ulcers, soft non-tender abdomen, no neurology deficit
data_table.Investigations: Point-of-care fourth-generation HIV antigen-antibody negative; FBC mild leucopenia 3.4 with mild thrombocytopenia 132; LFTs mildly elevated ALT 78; EBV serology and CMV pending; chlamydia, gonorrhoea NAAT and syphilis serology sent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation to confirm or exclude acute HIV seroconversion?

options.text: Reassure that the negative fourth-generation antigen-antibody test 3 weeks post-exposure effectively excludes HIV given that the p24 antigen detection window opens within 2 weeks, and discharge with safer-sex counselling and routine STI follow-up in 3 months
options.rationale: Fourth-generation tests may still be negative in early seroconversion if HIV RNA is still developing; HIV RNA (viral load) is needed to confirm. (Source: ASHM: Australian HIV Testing Guidelines)

options.text: Send Epstein-Barr virus and cytomegalovirus serology and a heterophile antibody test to confirm acute infectious mononucleosis as a more probable diagnosis given the lymphadenopathy and pharyngitis, with planned outpatient follow-up in 7 days
options.rationale: EBV/CMV should be considered but does not exclude acute HIV; HIV RNA is essential when seroconversion is clinically suspected. (Source: ASHM: Australian HIV Testing Guidelines)

options.text: Treat empirically for secondary syphilis with intramuscular benzathine penicillin 1.8 g stat given the rash and the high-risk exposure, with planned outpatient infectious diseases follow-up to confirm with syphilis serology and consider HIV testing in 3 months
options.rationale: Secondary syphilis can present similarly but does not exclude HIV; HIV RNA testing is essential when acute HIV is suspected. (Source: ASHM: Australian HIV Testing Guidelines)

options.text: Commence empirical post-exposure prophylaxis with tenofovir/emtricitabine and dolutegravir for 28 days given the high-risk exposure profile and the clinically suspected seroconversion, with planned repeat HIV testing at week 4 to confirm or exclude
options.rationale: PEP must be commenced within 72 hours of exposure; at 3 weeks post-exposure it is too late and the priority is to confirm or exclude established infection. (Source: ASHM: Australian HIV Testing Guidelines)

options.text: Send quantitative HIV RNA viral load testing along with fourth-generation antigen-antibody confirmation, refer urgently to specialist HIV outpatients for confirmation and ART initiation if positive, and counsel on partner notification and testing
options.rationale: When acute seroconversion is suspected and fourth-generation antigen-antibody is negative, HIV RNA viral load is the test of choice as RNA can be detected approximately 10 days post-exposure, before antibody seroconversion. (Source: ASHM: Australian HIV Testing Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Acute HIV seroconversion typically presents 2 to 4 weeks post-exposure with a flu-like illness, pharyngitis, lymphadenopathy, rash, mucocutaneous ulcers, and may show transient cytopenias and mild transaminitis. Fourth-generation antigen-antibody tests detect both p24 antigen and antibody, narrowing the window period to approximately 2 to 3 weeks, but may still be negative in very early seroconversion. HIV RNA (viral load) is detectable from approximately 10 days post-exposure and is the test of choice when acute seroconversion is suspected with a negative serology. Early diagnosis allows immediate ART initiation and partner notification.
key_points: Acute HIV: fevers, pharyngitis, lymphadenopathy, rash, mucocutaneous ulcers 2-4 weeks post-exposure
HIV RNA detectable approximately 10 days post-exposure
explanation.pearls: PEP must be commenced within 72 hours of exposure; PrEP is for ongoing risk reduction in HIV-negative individuals at high risk and is available on PBS in Australia for eligible patients.

subtopic_detail: Septic shock hour-1 bundle blood cultures antibiotics fluids lactate
stem: You are the emergency department registrar reviewing a 68-year-old man brought by ambulance with rigors, confusion and hypotension. He has a 2-day history of urinary frequency, dysuria and flank pain. He has type 2 diabetes mellitus on metformin and a history of benign prostatic hyperplasia. He is alert but disoriented, has dry mucous membranes and warm peripheries. There are no signs of obvious surgical abdomen, no obvious focal infection elsewhere, and no recent hospitalisation, urological instrumentation or known antibiotic-resistant organism colonisation. Three sets of blood cultures have been drawn and a peripheral large-bore intravenous line has been placed. His weight is 80 kg.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.6, HR 122, BP 85/45, RR 26, SpO2 95% room air, GCS 14
data_table.Examination: Warm peripheries, capillary refill 3 seconds, mildly tender right flank, soft abdomen, no peritonism, dry mucous membranes, no neurological focal deficit
data_table.Investigations: Venous lactate 5.2 mmol/L, WCC 19.4, CRP 256, creatinine 168 (baseline 88), urine dipstick LE+++ nit+ blood+, blood cultures sent, urine MCS sent, chest X-ray no consolidation

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management within the first hour?

options.text: Commence intravenous noradrenaline infusion through a central venous catheter at 5 micrograms per minute targeting a mean arterial pressure of 65 mmHg before commencing fluid resuscitation, with admission to the intensive care unit for ongoing management
options.rationale: Initial septic shock management starts with crystalloid resuscitation 30 mL/kg; vasopressors are added if hypotension persists, not before fluid loading. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Australian Adaptation)

options.text: Arrange urgent contrast-enhanced computed tomography of the abdomen and pelvis to identify the source of infection and any complicating obstructive uropathy or perinephric abscess prior to commencing antibiotic therapy or significant fluid resuscitation
options.rationale: Imaging must not delay antibiotics or fluids in septic shock; hour-1 bundle takes priority and imaging follows once stabilised. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Australian Adaptation)

options.text: Prescribe oral ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily for suspected uncomplicated urinary tract infection and discharge home with safety-net advice if blood pressure improves on a single litre normal saline bolus, with general practice review in 24 hours
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics and discharge are unsafe in septic shock with lactate 5.2 and hypotension; admission with IV therapy is required. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Australian Adaptation)

options.text: Hold antibiotics and fluids pending urine and blood culture sensitivities to avoid premature empirical antibiotic exposure that may compromise the bacteriological yield from cultures and broaden the patient's antibiotic resistance profile longer term
options.rationale: Delay of antibiotics in septic shock increases mortality by approximately 7% per hour; antibiotics must be commenced empirically within the first hour. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Australian Adaptation)

options.text: Commence intravenous antibiotics within 1 hour (gentamicin 5 mg/kg plus ampicillin 2 g for suspected urosepsis), administer 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid bolus over 1 to 3 hours, repeat lactate within 2 hours, source control planning and ICU referral
options.rationale: Surviving Sepsis hour-1 bundle: measure lactate, take blood cultures, administer broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour, commence 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate over 4 mmol/L, and apply vasopressors if persistent. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Australian Adaptation)

explanation.summary: The Surviving Sepsis Campaign hour-1 bundle (endorsed in Australian sepsis pathways) comprises five elements: measure lactate (and remeasure if over 2 mmol/L), obtain blood cultures before antibiotics, administer broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour, begin 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid for hypotension or lactate over 4 mmol/L within 3 hours, and apply vasopressors if hypotension persists during or after fluid resuscitation to target MAP of at least 65 mmHg. Empirical antibiotic choice is guided by suspected source: gentamicin plus ampicillin for community urosepsis. Source control is sought once stabilised.
key_points: Hour-1 bundle: lactate, cultures, antibiotics within 1 hour, 30 mL/kg crystalloid, vasopressors as needed
Antibiotic delay increases mortality approximately 7% per hour in septic shock
MAP target at least 65 mmHg with noradrenaline as first-line vasopressor
explanation.pearls: Balanced crystalloids (Hartmann's or Plasma-Lyte) are preferred over normal saline in sepsis to reduce hyperchloraemic acidosis and acute kidney injury, per the SMART and SALT-ED trials.
